title: Play Kitchen Stove and Sink with Running Water
summary: A matching pair of toy kitchen base cabinets — a sink unit built around a purchased running-water toy sink, and a stove unit with an acrylic "glass" cooktop — each with a hinged cabinet door below.
description:
This is genuinely two builds sharing one construction method — a sink base and a stove base, sized around their specific inserts. Measure your own sink and adjust the plan as needed before cutting anything; the cut list is built around a sink roughly 16" x 10-3/4", and a different model will shift several dimensions.

Build both bases the same way: sides to toekick, front to sides, bottom shelf, back top support and interior shelf, then the back panel — nailed and glued at every joint, no screws needed in the case itself. The doors are the one place accuracy matters most: build each door frame to the actual opening (not a fixed number), keep the plywood door-back panel about 1" larger than the frame's center opening, and hang with surface-mount hinges and a magnetic clasp so a toddler can actually open it.

materials.name: Pine, 1x3, 8 ft
materials.note: Toekicks, back top supports, and door frames for both units.

materials.name: 1/4" plywood, 2 ft x 2 ft plus scrap for door backs
Toy sink with running water, about 16" x 10-3/4"

cutList.part: Sink toekick and back top support

cutList.material: 1/4" plywood, 2 ft x 2 ft plus scrap for door backs

cutList.part: Stove toekick and back top support

cutList.material: 1/4" plywood, 2 ft x 2 ft plus scrap for door backs

steps.title: Measure your sink, then cut the parts
steps.body: Measure your actual sink before cutting anything — the cut list is built around one roughly 16" x 10-3/4", and a different model shifts the top frame and the case width with it. For the sink unit cut from the 5 ft 1x12 two sides at 19-1/4" and a bottom at 16-1/2"; for the stove unit cut from the 6 ft 1x12 two sides at 19-1/4", a bottom at 13-1/2" and the 15" top. Cut the 1x3 toekick and back top support at 16-1/2" for the sink and 13-1/2" for the stove, the 1x4 fronts at 18" and 15", the 1x10 shelves at 16-1/2" and 13-1/2", and the 1x2 top pieces at 18" and 9-3/4" for the sink and 15" for the stove. Cut the two plywood backs 18" x 19-1/4" and 15" x 19-1/4". Set the saw once per length and cut both units' matching parts together — they are the same cabinet at two widths, and matching cuts are what make them read as a pair.
materials: 1/4" plywood, 2 ft x 2 ft plus scrap for door backs

steps.title: Attach the sides to the toekick
steps.body: Work one unit at a time, but build both the same way. Stand the two 19-1/4" sides on edge and set the toekick between them at the bottom, flush with the front edges — 16-1/2" on the sink, 13-1/2" on the stove, the same length as that unit's bottom shelf. Glue every mating face and drive 1-1/4" brad nails through the sides into the ends of the toekick; there are no screws anywhere in these cases, so the glue does the real work and the nails are just clamps holding it while it cures.

steps.title: Attach the front
steps.body: Set the 1x4 front across the tops of the two sides — 18" on the sink, 15" on the stove, each the case's full outside width — flush at the top and flush with the outside face of each side, so it caps the case and squares it. Glue and pin with 1-1/4" brads. Compare the diagonals of the front opening now: with only the toekick and this front holding the sides, it is the last easy moment to push a rack out of the case.

steps.title: Attach the bottom shelf
steps.body: Drop the bottom shelf in on top of the toekick — 16-1/2" for the sink, 13-1/2" for the stove, cut to sit between the sides — and glue and nail it through the sides and up through the toekick with 1-1/4" brads. It should sit level and tight down on the toekick's top edge along its whole length; a shelf carried by glue at one end only is where a toy cabinet fails the first time a child leans on the open door.

steps.title: Add the back top support and interior shelf
steps.body: Glue and pin the 1x3 back top support across the back top corner, between the sides and flush with their back edges — it ties the two sides together at the top and gives the back panel something to land on. Then set the 1x10 interior shelf in flush to the back, leaving about 6" of space under it as the plan specifies, and glue and nail through the sides into its ends. Sight along its front edge and check it runs parallel to the bottom shelf before the glue grabs.

steps.title: Attach the back panel
steps.body: Lay each case face down and set its 1/4" plywood back on — 18" x 19-1/4" for the sink, 15" x 19-1/4" for the stove. Line the panel up with one side and the top edge and tack those, then push the case until it meets the panel's other two edges before nailing the rest with 5/8" brads and glue. A square back panel is what holds a glued-and-nailed case square permanently, so spend the extra minute here.
materials: 1/4" plywood, 2 ft x 2 ft plus scrap for door backs

steps.title: Build and attach the tops
steps.body: The sink top is a frame, not a panel: join the two 18" 1x2 front and back pieces to the two 9-3/4" side pieces with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws, sides captured between front and back so the frame finishes 18" x 11-1/4" — the same footprint as the case. Test the sink in the frame's opening before you fasten anything: as dimensioned the opening is smaller than the sink the material list names, so this is where you widen the frame to your own sink rather than after it is glued down. Glue and pin the frame to the sink case with 1-1/4" brads, then drop the sink in. The stove's top is the solid 15" x 11-1/4" panel with its 15" 1x2 trim along the back edge, glued and nailed on the same way.
materials: Toy sink with running water, about 16" x 10-3/4"

steps.title: Build and hang the doors
steps.body: Measure each finished door opening directly and build its 1x3 frame to fit — there is no cut-list size for the doors on purpose, because a nailed-together case is never exactly the plan's number. Assemble each frame flat so it doesn't wind, corners joined with 3/4" pocket holes and two 1-1/4" pocket hole screws per joint. Glue and pin a piece of 1/4" plywood to the back of the frame with 5/8" brads, sized about 1" larger than the frame's center opening so it laps roughly 1/2" onto the frame all round rather than covering the whole door. On the stove, bed the 9" x 11" acrylic cooktop on the top panel with a suitable glue, or screw it through predrilled holes — drill acrylic slowly and it won't crack. Hang each door on surface mount hinges and add a magnetic clasp, then test it with one finger: a toddler has to be able to open it and it has to stay shut on its own.
materials: 1/4" plywood, 2 ft x 2 ft plus scrap for door backs

images.alt: A matching toy kitchen sink and stove base cabinet, each with a hinged door
